parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I walked into the grocery store yesterday and just stood frozen in the doorway for a solid minute. It used to be completely fine, but suddenly the fluorescent lights felt way too bright and the background chatter was overwhelming. My chest got tight and I had to grip the cart handle just to steady myself. I ended up grabbing a few things and rushing back out before I could even finish my list. It really caught me off guard how quickly my whole body reacted to something so ordinary.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really unsettling — especially because it came out of nowhere in a place that used to feel normal. What you’re describing sounds a lot like your nervous system went into a kind of overload response: the bright lights, the noise, and the pressure of being there may have hit all at once, and your body reacted before your mind could catch up.

A few things can help in the moment if this happens again:
- pause and do a quick grounding check: name 5 things you see, 4 you feel, 3 you hear
- slow your breathing a little, especially making the exhale longer than the inhale
- step to the edge of the store or outside for a minute if you can
- lower sensory input if possible, like sunglasses, earbuds, or a quieter time of day

It may also help to notice whether you were already stressed, tired, hungry, or overstimulated before going in, because those can make an ordinary place suddenly feel too much.

If this starts happening more often, or you’re noticing chest tightness and strong panic-like reactions in other settings too, it could be worth talking to a doctor or mental health professional just to rule out anything medical and to get support.

If you want, I can help you think through what might have triggered it, or help you make a plan for getting through grocery trips more comfortably.

turns.user_message: Since that trip, I keep finding myself hesitating right outside the automatic doors every single time I go out. What used to be a quick errand now feels like I am walking into a storm. I spend so much energy just trying to pace my breathing before I even step over the threshold. My heart starts pounding the second I hear the hum of the freezers, and I catch myself scanning for the nearest exit. It is exhausting to feel like my own nervous system is constantly on high alert over something so routine.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really exhausting and scary. It makes sense that your body is reacting this way if something about that trip left you feeling overwhelmed or unsafe — sometimes our nervous system keeps sounding the alarm long after the moment has passed.

What you’re describing sounds a lot like a learned fear response: your body has started associating those doors, sounds, and the whole “going in” moment with danger. That doesn’t mean you’re broken, and it doesn’t mean this has to stay this intense forever.

A few things that might help in the moment:

- **Pause before the threshold** and orient yourself: name 3 things you see, 2 things you hear, 1 thing you can touch.
- **Slow the exhale** a little more than the inhale, if you can. Even a few longer exhales can help tell your body you’re not in immediate danger.
- **Make a tiny entry plan**: “I only need to go in, get one thing, and leave.”
- **Ground with your body**: press your feet into the floor, unclench your jaw, relax your shoulders.
- **Bring a support if possible** for a little while, so the place feels less loaded.

If this keeps happening, or it’s getting worse, it could be really worth talking to a therapist or another mental health professional. You deserve help with this, especially if it’s starting to shrink your world.

If you want, I can also help you figure out what happened on that trip, or help you build a step-by-step plan for getting through store entrances with less panic.

turns.user_message: Now I avoid going anywhere with too many people because the panic just hits me out of nowhere. I stood near the entrance again last night and my vision actually started to blur while my hands went completely numb. I had to lean against the wall and count backwards just to keep from bolting right out the door. It feels like my brain is stuck in permanent overdrive, turning every crowded space into a minefield I cannot navigate. I am so tired of feeling trapped in my own head whenever I try to do normal things.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really frightening and exhausting. I’m sorry you’re going through this. What you described—blurred vision, numb hands, feeling like you have to escape—can happen during intense panic, and it makes sense that you’d start avoiding crowded places after experiences like that.

A few things that may help in the moment:
- **Slow your breathing**: try inhaling for 4, exhaling for 6, and repeat a few times.
- **Grounding**: name 5 things you can see, 4 you can feel, 3 you can hear.
- **Reduce the “must escape now” feeling**: remind yourself, “This is panic. It will peak and pass.”
- **Have an exit plan**: standing near an entrance, taking a friend, or choosing a quieter spot can help.

Because this is starting to limit your life, it would be a really good idea to talk with a **mental health professional or a doctor**. Panic attacks are treatable, and you don’t have to manage this alone. If you ever have **chest pain, fainting, trouble breathing, or symptoms that feel different from your usual panic**, seek urgent medical care to be safe.

If you want, I can also help you make a simple “panic plan” for crowded places that you can keep on your phone.
